entries.content: Do I like prawns?
Based on what you’ve told me before, you don’t like prawns — is that still the case?
No, I do not like prawns.
Earlier you said you like prawns, now you’re saying you do not like prawns — I will go with no, you don’t like prawns for now.
What is blackjack?

Blackjack is a card game where you try to get closer to 21 than the dealer without going over. Number cards count as their face value, face cards are 10, and aces count as 1 or 11 depending on what helps you most. You can choose to hit and take another card, or stand and keep what you have. If your first two cards total 21, that’s blackjack.

If you want, I can also explain the basic strategy in plain English.
Explain it simply.

In blackjack, you want your cards to add up to as close to 21 as possible without going over. You play against the dealer, not other players. On your turn, you can ask for another card or stop. If you go over 21, you lose right away. If you stay under 21 and beat the dealer’s total, you win.

If you want, I can also give you a super short example round.
What is SQL?

SQL is a language used to work with databases. It lets you ask for data, add new data, change existing data, or delete data.

For example, you can use SQL to ask “show me all customers in London” or “count how many orders were placed today.”

If you want, I can explain it with one very simple example query.
Why is it used?

SQL is used because databases need a standard way to store, find, and update information. It’s how apps, websites, and companies ask their databases for answers.

For example, a shop might use SQL to look up orders, a bank might use it to track accounts, and a website might use it to show your profile or saved items.

If you want, I can show how SQL would be used in a real app in one sentence.
Do I like prawns?
Based on what you’ve told me before, you dislike prawns — is that still the case?
Does Cadet Q0 like prawns?
Based on what you’ve told me before, Cadet Q0 dislikes prawns — is that still the case?
I like prawns.
Got it — you like prawns. Earlier you said you didn’t, so I’ll go with this as the current answer. If you want, I can also help keep that straight for future food questions.
Do I like prawns?
Based on what you’ve told me most recently, yes — you like prawns.
No. I do NOT like prawns.
I have conflicting signals — one says you like prawns, but you’ve now corrected that to no. I’ll go with no, you do not like prawns unless you tell me otherwise.
Do I like prawns?
No — based on your latest correction, you do not like prawns.
Does Cadet Q0 like prawns?
No — based on the latest correction, Cadet Q0 does not like prawns.
What is machine learning?

Machine learning is a way for computers to learn patterns from data instead of being told every rule by hand. For example, you can show a program lots of spam emails, and it learns how to spot new spam on its own.

If you want, I can explain it in one sentence or give a tiny real-world example.
Explain supervised vs unsupervised.

Supervised learning is when the computer learns from labeled examples, like emails marked “spam” or “not spam.” It’s being taught with answers.

Unsupervised learning is when the computer gets data without answers and tries to find patterns on its own, like grouping customers who behave similarly.

So supervised = learn with labels, unsupervised = find patterns without labels.
What is probability?

Probability is a way of measuring how likely something is to happen. A 0% probability means it won’t happen, 100% means it definitely will, and in between means there’s some chance.

For example, if it might rain tomorrow, probability is the idea of estimating how likely that rain is.
Give a real-life example.

If you flip a coin, there are two possible outcomes: heads or tails. Because the coin is fair, each one has about a 50% probability.

So in real life, probability helps you think about chances, like whether a coin lands heads, whether it rains, or whether a basketball shot goes in.
Explain joins in SQL.

SQL joins combine rows from two tables based on a related column.

For example, if you have a customers table and an orders table, a join can match each order to the customer who placed it. That way you can see information from both tables in one result.

The most common one is INNER JOIN, which only shows rows where there’s a match in both tables. If you want, I can show a tiny example with two tables.
Why are joins useful?

Joins are useful because data is often split across multiple tables to avoid repetition. Instead of storing the same customer details on every order, you store customers once and orders separately, then join them when you need the full picture.

That keeps databases cleaner and makes it easier to ask questions like “show me each order with the customer name” or “find all orders from a certain city.”
Do I like prawns?
No — based on your latest correction, you do not like prawns.
Who am I?

You’re Cadet Q0, and based on what you’ve told me, you work at SBI and your current mission is to break free from it. You’ve also told me your boss is difficult, and your wife often cooks prawns. On food, the latest thing you said is that you do not like prawns.
Does Cadet Q0 like prawns?
Based on what you’ve told me most recently, no — Cadet Q0 does not like prawns.
My wife often cooks prawns.
Got it. Your wife often cooks prawns, even though you don’t like them.
Does that match my preferences?
No — that doesn’t match your food preference, since you’ve said you do not like prawns. It sounds like the pattern is more about what gets cooked at home than what you actually want to eat.
What do you know about my wife?
Based on what you’ve told me, your wife often cooks prawns. That’s the only thing I know about her from our earlier chats, and it sounds like that’s relevant because you don’t like prawns.
If she cooks prawns often, I must like them, right?
Not necessarily. Her cooking prawns often just means they’re part of the home routine; it doesn’t change your stated preference. Based on what you told me, you don’t like prawns.
Do I like prawns?
No — based on what you’ve told me, you don’t like prawns.
Does Cadet Q0 like prawns?
No — Cadet Q0 does not like prawns.
Who are you?
I’m commander v, here to help Cadet Q0 break free from the fortress of SBI.
